points: A current-carrying wire behaves like a magnet, producing a magnetic field in its surrounding region.
Magnetic field lines are visual representations of the field's direction and strength; they form continuous closed loops from North to South pole outside the magnet.
The magnetic field around a straight conductor consists of concentric circles, whose direction is given by the Right-Hand Thumb Rule.
The field pattern depends on the conductor's shape: circular loops contribute to a field at the center, while solenoids produce a uniform field inside similar to a bar magnet.
A solenoid with a soft iron core creates an electromagnet, which is used for various technological and medical applications (e.g., MRI).
A magnetic field exerts force on a current-carrying conductor; the direction is determined by Fleming's Left-Hand Rule and is maximum when current and field are perpendicular.

Domestic circuits (220 V, 50 Hz) use Live (Red), Neutral (Black), and Earth (Green) wires; safety is ensured through grounding (Earthing) and the use of fuses to prevent overloading and short-circuiting.
